claim: For company-scale AI agents, memory isolation becomes complex when one agent serves many users across channels (DMs, public channels, threads). Leakage prevention requires scoping contexts by user/channel to prevent cross-team data exposure.
actionable: Implement channel/user-level access controls: ensure an agent's context from the growth channel doesn't leak to engineering, and DMs stay private unless the user is from that team.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack

claim: Agent personality/tone matters significantly in company adoption. When testing a cheaper model as a replacement, users notice and resist personality changes even if tool performance is identical.
actionable: When comparing models for production, test with users on personality/tone, not just capability benchmarks. Preserve model choice if users have bonded with a particular style.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack

claim: Company AI agents need shared context from integrations (e.g., Meta Ads, Slack tools). Having one person connect an integration once and sharing it team-wide is simpler and safer than requiring each team member to connect individually.
actionable: Centralize integration connections: have admins connect once, then grant the agent access scoped to team needs. Avoid requiring individual users to connect.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack

claim: Slack as the interface for company agents works because: (1) feels like a teammate (no separate UI), (2) long-running tasks (10 min) feel fast compared to human expectations vs. web-app context switching.
actionable: Design agent UX in the user's native communication tool (Slack, Teams, etc.) to reduce friction and align latency expectations.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack

claim: Slack's complexity for agents includes handling edits, deletions, emoji reactions, thread drifts, and DM-to-thread transitions. These require explicit state management to avoid context leakage between conversation modes.
actionable: When building Slack agents, explicitly handle message edits (respond), deletions (stop), emoji reactions, thread context transitions, and DM continuity by rolling over previous conversation state.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack

claim: Building a scalable AI coworker requires three pillars: (1) helps get work done (models capable, integrations connected), (2) knows the company (uses Slack context well, passes Slack approval), (3) is friendly (personality matters for adoption).
actionable: Evaluate AI coworker implementations across three dimensions: task capability, company context awareness, and user-facing personality/tone.
source_title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack
